articles.title: Sequoyah and the invention of the syllabary
articles.body:
Around 1809, Sequoyah (ᏍᏏᏉᏯ) — a Cherokee silversmith who could not read or write any language — began working on a writing system for Cherokee. After experimenting with a symbol for every word, he arrived at a syllabary: one character per syllable, 85 in the final form.
He taught it first to his daughter Ayoka, and in 1821 demonstrated it to Cherokee leaders by having them dictate messages that Ayoka, out of earshot, read back perfectly. Within a few years an extraordinary share of the Cherokee Nation could read and write — a literacy rate that surpassed that of their settler neighbors.
Sequoyah is one of very few people in recorded history to invent a complete writing system from scratch while being unable to read any existing one. The giant sequoia tree is named in his honor.

articles.title: The Cherokee Phoenix
articles.body:
In 1828 the Cherokee Nation began publishing the **Cherokee Phoenix** (ᏣᎳᎩ ᏧᎴᎯᏌᏅᎯ, Tsalagi Tsulehisanvhi) — the first newspaper published by a Native nation in North America, printed side-by-side in Cherokee syllabary and English.
The Phoenix carried national news, laws, and editorials defending Cherokee sovereignty in the years before removal. It still publishes today, in print and online, and remains a living example of the syllabary in daily use.

articles.title: Gadugi — working together
articles.body:
**ᎦᏚᎩ** (gadugi) names a core Cherokee value: a group of people coming together to do work for the common good — planting a neighbor's field, repairing an elder's roof, cooking for a funeral.
Historically gadugi crews were organized, standing groups within a town. Today the word appears everywhere from tribal programs to software projects as shorthand for cooperative labor freely given. If you learn one cultural concept alongside the language, make it this one.

articles.title: Selu and Kanati
articles.body:
In Cherokee tradition, **Selu** (ᏎᎷ, Corn) is the first woman and **Kanati** (ᎧᎾᏘ, the Lucky Hunter) the first man. Their story — including the mischief of their sons, the Thunder Boys — explains the origin of corn and game, and why food must be treated with respect rather than taken for granted.
Corn, beans, and squash, planted together so each supports the others, anchored Cherokee farming for centuries. The Green Corn Ceremony, held when the new corn ripens, remains one of the most important traditional observances — a time of renewal, forgiveness, and gratitude.

articles.title: The Cherokee Nation today
articles.body:
Cherokee people today belong to three federally recognized tribes: the **Cherokee Nation** and the **United Keetoowah Band of Cherokee Indians**, both headquartered in Tahlequah, Oklahoma, and the **Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians** in Cherokee, North Carolina.
The language is critically endangered — first-language speakers number only in the low thousands, most of them elders — and all three tribes run intensive revitalization efforts: immersion schools where children learn every subject in Cherokee, master-apprentice programs pairing learners with fluent elders, and the addition of the syllabary to Unicode, which is why this course can display ᏣᎳᎩ on your screen.
Learning even a little of the language as an outsider is welcomed by many, but comes with a responsibility: defer to Cherokee communities as the owners of the language, and support their programs where you can.
